Árboles

library(GGally)
## Loading required package: ggplot2
library(nortest)
library(dplyr)
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
library(tidyverse)
## ── Attaching core tidyverse packages ──────────────────────── tidyverse 2.0.0 ──
## ✔ forcats   1.0.1     ✔ stringr   1.6.0
## ✔ lubridate 1.9.4     ✔ tibble    3.3.1
## ✔ purrr     1.2.1     ✔ tidyr     1.3.2
## ✔ readr     2.1.6
## ── Conflicts ────────────────────────────────────────── tidyverse_conflicts() ──
## ✖ dplyr::filter() masks stats::filter()
## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()
## ℹ Use the conflicted package (<http://conflicted.r-lib.org/>) to force all conflicts to become errors
library(rpart)
library(caret)
## Loading required package: lattice
## 
## Attaching package: 'caret'
## 
## The following object is masked from 'package:purrr':
## 
##     lift
library(discretization)
library(rpart.plot)
load("datos/listings_modificado.RData")
ls()
## [1] "listings"
listings$log_price <- log(listings$price)
summary(listings$log_price)
##    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
##   2.079   4.787   5.263   5.381   5.787  10.822
# 1. Calcular los cortes (percentiles 33% y 66%) para dividir en 3 partes equitativas
cortes <- quantile(listings$price, probs = c(0.33, 0.66), na.rm = TRUE)

# 2. Crear la nueva variable categórica y convertirla a factor
listings <- listings %>%
  mutate(
    categoria_precio = case_when(
      price <= cortes[1] ~ "Económica",
      price > cortes[1] & price <= cortes[2] ~ "Intermedia",
      price > cortes[2] ~ "Cara"
    ),
    categoria_precio = as.factor(categoria_precio)
  )

# 3. Mostrar cuántas propiedades quedaron en cada categoría para el informe
table(listings$categoria_precio)
## 
##       Cara  Económica Intermedia 
##      25897      25185      25164
categoricas <- c(
  "room_type",
  "property_type",
  "neighbourhood",
  "city",
  "host_is_superhost",
  "instant_bookable",
  "bathroom_type"
)

listings[categoricas] <- lapply(listings[categoricas], as.factor)
set.seed(123)

n <- nrow(listings)
train_data <- sample(1:n, size = 0.7*n)

train <- listings[train_data, ]
test <- listings[-train_data, ]
set.seed(123)

sample_index <- sample(1:nrow(listings), 5000)
listings_sample <- listings[sample_index, ]


arbol1 <- rpart(price ~ 
  accommodates +
  bathrooms_num +
  bedrooms +
  beds +
  host_response_rate +
  host_acceptance_rate +
  calculated_host_listings_count +
  number_of_reviews +
  reviews_per_month +
  review_scores_rating +
  minimum_nights +
  availability_365 +
  room_type +
  host_is_superhost +
  instant_bookable +
  bathroom_type,
  data = listings_sample)

rpart.plot(arbol1)

Fue necesario escoger las variables categóricas más importantes y trabajar con muestra porque la cantidad de datos generaba que RStudio se detuviera por un stack overflow.

Se puede observar que las variables más importantes son: room_type availability_365 beds calculated_host_listings_count

Otras variables como minimum_nights y host_acceptance_rate también son importantes, aunque no tanto como las anteriores.

modelo1 <- rpart(price ~ 
  accommodates +
  bathrooms_num +
  bedrooms +
  beds +
  host_response_rate +
  host_acceptance_rate +
  calculated_host_listings_count +
  number_of_reviews +
  reviews_per_month +
  review_scores_rating +
  minimum_nights +
  availability_365 +
  room_type +
  host_is_superhost +
  instant_bookable +
  bathroom_type,
  data = train)

rpart.plot(modelo1)

#Predicción en test
predModelo1 <- predict(modelo1, newdata = test)

#Métricas en test
rmseModelo1test <- RMSE(predModelo1, test$price)
mseModelo1test <- mean((predModelo1 - test$price)^2)

#Predicción en train
predModelo1Train <- predict(modelo1, newdata = train)

#Métricas en train
rmseModelo1train <- RMSE(predModelo1Train, train$price)
mseModelo1train <- mean((predModelo1Train - train$price)^2)

#Resultados
rmseModelo1test
## [1] 2478.741
mseModelo1test
## [1] 6144158
rmseModelo1train
## [1] 2351.682
mseModelo1train
## [1] 5530409
#Error absoluto
abs(rmseModelo1test - rmseModelo1train) / rmseModelo1train * 100
## [1] 5.402901

El error nos dice que el modelo es estable la mayoría de los casos y no hay overfitting.

plot(test$price, col="blue", main="Predicciones vs valores originales")

points(predModelo1, col="red")

legend("topright",
       legend=c("original", "prediccion"),
       col=c("blue", "red"),
       pch=1,
       cex=0.8)

En este gráfico parece pasar lo mismo que en el ejemplo de clase, al modelo le cuesta mucho predecir los valores altos, puede que sean outliers o que se arregle al probar luego con los distintos modelos adaptados.

controlcv <- trainControl(method = "cv", number = 5)

profundidades <- data.frame(maxdepth = c(2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10))

modelo_depth <- train(price ~ 
  accommodates +
  bathrooms_num +
  bedrooms +
  beds +
  host_response_rate +
  host_acceptance_rate +
  calculated_host_listings_count +
  number_of_reviews +
  reviews_per_month +
  review_scores_rating +
  minimum_nights +
  availability_365 +
  room_type +
  host_is_superhost +
  instant_bookable +
  bathroom_type,
  data = train,
  method = "rpart2",
  tuneGrid = profundidades,
  trControl = controlcv,
  metric = "RMSE"
)
modelo_depth
## CART 
## 
## 53372 samples
##    16 predictor
## 
## No pre-processing
## Resampling: Cross-Validated (5 fold) 
## Summary of sample sizes: 42699, 42697, 42698, 42697, 42697 
## Resampling results across tuning parameters:
## 
##   maxdepth  RMSE      Rsquared   MAE     
##    2        3231.797  0.4217485  685.4625
##    3        3231.797  0.4217485  685.4625
##    4        2986.673  0.5081097  619.5279
##    5        2874.731  0.5437809  591.5201
##    6        2717.228  0.5892891  574.8577
##    8        2608.750  0.6208590  544.1955
##   10        2515.298  0.6477190  510.9392
## 
## RMSE was used to select the optimal model using the smallest value.
## The final value used for the model was maxdepth = 10.
#Gráfico de RMSE vs profundidad
plot(modelo_depth)

Modelos con diferentes profundidades:

modelo2 <- rpart(price ~ 
  accommodates +
  bathrooms_num +
  bedrooms +
  beds +
  host_response_rate +
  host_acceptance_rate +
  calculated_host_listings_count +
  number_of_reviews +
  reviews_per_month +
  review_scores_rating +
  minimum_nights +
  availability_365 +
  room_type +
  host_is_superhost +
  instant_bookable +
  bathroom_type,
  data = train,
  method = "anova",
  control = rpart.control(maxdepth = 3)
)

rpart.plot(modelo2)

#Predicción en test
predModelo2 <- predict(modelo2, newdata = test)

#Métricas en test
rmseModelo2test <- RMSE(predModelo2, test$price)
mseModelo2test <- mean((predModelo2 - test$price)^2)

#Predicción en train
predModelo2Train <- predict(modelo2, newdata = train)

#Métricas en train
rmseModelo2train <- RMSE(predModelo2Train, train$price)
mseModelo2train <- mean((predModelo2Train - train$price)^2)

#Resultados
rmseModelo2test
## [1] 3075.267
mseModelo2test
## [1] 9457267
rmseModelo2train
## [1] 2982.862
mseModelo2train
## [1] 8897466
#Error absoluto
abs(rmseModelo2test - rmseModelo2train) / rmseModelo2train * 100
## [1] 3.097857
plot(test$price, col="blue", main="Predicciones vs valores originales")

points(predModelo2, col="red")

legend("topright",
       legend=c("original", "prediccion"),
       col=c("blue", "red"),
       pch=1,
       cex=0.8)

modelo3 <- rpart(price ~ 
  accommodates +
  bathrooms_num +
  bedrooms +
  beds +
  host_response_rate +
  host_acceptance_rate +
  calculated_host_listings_count +
  number_of_reviews +
  reviews_per_month +
  review_scores_rating +
  minimum_nights +
  availability_365 +
  room_type +
  host_is_superhost +
  instant_bookable +
  bathroom_type,
  data = train,
  method = "anova",
  control = rpart.control(maxdepth = 6)
)

rpart.plot(modelo3)

#Predicción en test
predModelo3 <- predict(modelo3, newdata = test)

#Métricas en test
rmseModelo3test <- RMSE(predModelo3, test$price)
mseModelo3test <- mean((predModelo3 - test$price)^2)

#Predicción en train
predModelo3Train <- predict(modelo3, newdata = train)

#Métricas en train
rmseModelo3train <- RMSE(predModelo3Train, train$price)
mseModelo3train <- mean((predModelo3Train - train$price)^2)

#Resultados
rmseModelo3test
## [1] 2478.741
mseModelo3test
## [1] 6144158
rmseModelo3train
## [1] 2351.682
mseModelo3train
## [1] 5530409
#Error absoluto
abs(rmseModelo3test - rmseModelo3train) / rmseModelo3train * 100
## [1] 5.402901
plot(test$price, col="blue", main="Predicciones vs valores originales")

points(predModelo3, col="red")

legend("topright",
       legend=c("original", "prediccion"),
       col=c("blue", "red"),
       pch=1,
       cex=0.8)

modelo4 <- rpart(price ~ 
  accommodates +
  bathrooms_num +
  bedrooms +
  beds +
  host_response_rate +
  host_acceptance_rate +
  calculated_host_listings_count +
  number_of_reviews +
  reviews_per_month +
  review_scores_rating +
  minimum_nights +
  availability_365 +
  room_type +
  host_is_superhost +
  instant_bookable +
  bathroom_type,
  data = train,
  method = "anova",
  control = rpart.control(maxdepth = 9)
)

rpart.plot(modelo4)

#Predicción en test
predModelo4 <- predict(modelo4, newdata = test)

#Métricas en test
rmseModelo4test <- RMSE(predModelo4, test$price)
mseModelo4test <- mean((predModelo4 - test$price)^2)

#Predicción en train
predModelo4Train <- predict(modelo4, newdata = train)

#Métricas en train
rmseModelo4train <- RMSE(predModelo4Train, train$price)
mseModelo4train <- mean((predModelo4Train - train$price)^2)

#Resultados
rmseModelo4test
## [1] 2478.741
mseModelo4test
## [1] 6144158
rmseModelo4train
## [1] 2351.682
mseModelo4train
## [1] 5530409
#Error absoluto
abs(rmseModelo4test - rmseModelo4train) / rmseModelo4train * 100
## [1] 5.402901
plot(test$price, col="blue", main="Predicciones vs valores originales")

points(predModelo3, col="red")

legend("topright",
       legend=c("original", "prediccion"),
       col=c("blue", "red"),
       pch=1,
       cex=0.8)
